title: Case on Impeachment Trial of Prime Minister Serving as Acting President
text:
1Case on Impeachment Trial of Prime Minister Serving as Acting President
[2024Hun-Na9, March 24, 2025]
* First Draft
This case concerns the first constitutional adjudication in the history of the Republic involving an impeachment 
trial against a “Prime Minister serving as Acting President.” The Court issued a decision rejecting the claim for 
removal from office in accordance with the main clause of Article 23, Section (2) of the Constitutional Court 
Act, with five Justices expressing opinions to reject, one to uphold, and two to dismiss.
Background of the Case
On December 14, 2024, at its 4th plenary of the 419th session (extraordinary), the National 
Assembly passed an impeachment resolution against President Yoon Suk-yeol, whereupon 
Respondent began to exercise the powers of the President in an acting capacity on the same 
day.
On December 26, 2024, 170 members of the National Assembly proposed an 
“impeachment resolution against Prime Minister Han Duck-soo” on the grounds, inter alia, 
that Respondent violated the Constitution and statutes by condoning and failing to prevent the 
President’s abuse of the veto power over legislation and by refusing, as Acting President, to 
appoint three Constitutional Court Justices elected by the National Assembly.
On December 27, 2024, at its 2nd plenary of the 420th session (extraordinary), the 
National Assembly passed the impeachment resolution against Respondent by 192 votes in 
favor out of 192 votes cast. On the same day, the impeachment prosecutor, pursuant to 
Article 49, Section (2) of the Constitutional Court Act, filed the instant petition for an 
impeachment trial by submitting the original copy of the impeachment resolution to the Court.
Subject Matter of Review
2The subject matter of review in this case is whether Prime Minister Han Duck-soo violated 
the Constitution or statutes in the course of performing his official duties and whether a 
decision to remove him from office should be rendered.
Summary of the Decision
1. Discussion of justiciability
The proviso to Article 65, Section (2) of the Constitution requires a supermajority vote—
the concurrence of two-thirds of the total members of the National Assembly —for the 
impeachment of the President, in order to ensure that impeachment is exercised with caution 
in light of, inter alia, the President’s significant democratic legitimacy as both head of state 
and head of the executive branch (Article 66, Sections (1) and (4)) and the importance of the 
President’s constitutional status and authority. By contrast, although the Prime Minister is 
appointed by the President with the consent of the National Assembly pursuant to Article 86 
of the Constitution, the Prime Minister possesses only considerably reduced, indirect 
democratic legitimacy compared to that of the President —who is directly elected by the 
people —and therefore occupies a distinctly different status from the President when serving 
as Acting President. Considering this distinction together with the purpose of the 
impeachment system, which is to safeguard the Constitution through the removal of a public 
official from office, it is reasonable to apply the voting requirement under the main clause of 
Article 65, Section (2) of the Constitution to the impeachment of a Prime Minister serving as 
Acting President, by reference to the original status as Prime Minister.
As the impeachment resolution against Respondent was passed with the approval of a 
majority of the total members of the National Assembly, the instant petition for an 
impeachment trial, based thereon, is justiciable. 
2. Discussion of the merits
 A. Opinion of rejection by Justices Moon Hyungbae, Lee Mison, Kim Hyungdu, and Jung 
Jungmi
(1) Whether there was a violation of the Constitution or statutes
3(a) Regarding the exercise of the authority to request reconsideration of bills on the 
appointment of a special prosecutor
In light of, inter alia, the President’s broad discretion in exercising the authority to request 
reconsideration of bills and the constitutional status and character of the Prime Minister and 
the State Council, it is difficult to conclude ––absent special circumstances such as 
Respondent actively pressing or recommending that the President exercise such authority ––
that Respondent substantially influenced, encouraged, or condoned the exercise of this 
presidential prerogative merely by presiding over State Council meetings that adopted 
resolutions requesting reconsideration of bills on the appointment of a special prosecutor. 
According to the record of this case, there is no evidence or objective material indicating 
that Respondent, as Prime Minister, actively pressed or recommended that the President 
exercise the authority to request reconsideration of bills, or that Respondent encouraged or 
condoned an abuse of such authority by the President. Therefore, we cannot find that 
Respondent violated, inter alia, Articles 7, 40, 49, and 86 of the Constitution. 
(b) Regarding the declaration of martial law and act of insurrection
According to the record of this case, there is no evidence or objective material indicating 
that Respondent took active steps, such as recommending the convening of the State Council, 
in order to confer procedural legitimacy upon the declaration of martial law. Nor is there 
evidence or objective material establishing the facts alleged in the impeachment petition, such 
as Respondent’s failure to recommend the convening of the State Council to the President 
after the National Assembly adopted a resolution demanding the termination of martial law. 
Therefore, we cannot find that Respondent violated, inter alia, Articles 7 and 86 of the 
Constitution and Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act.
(c) Regarding the joint administration of State affairs 
We interpret that the overall tenor of Respondent’s statement was to express to the people 
the determination of the administration and the ruling party to cooperate and make every 
effort to restore public confidence and stability in the aftermath of the declaration and 
termination of martial law. It cannot be regarded as intending to negate the presidential 
system of government, which is founded on the “principle of independence” between the 
4executive and the legislature. Nor is there any direct evidence or example indicating that 
Respondent jointly governed with the leader of the ruling party on the basis of this statement. 
Therefore, we cannot find that Respondent violated, inter alia, Article 7, Article 66, Section 
(4), and Article 74, Section (1) of the Constitution.
(d) Regarding the request for recommendation of special prosecutor candidates 
The actual period during which Respondent, as Acting President, did not request the 
recommendation of special prosecutor candidates was only about ten days. Since the 
composition of the Special Prosecutor Candidate Recommendation Committee was based on 
the “Rule on the Composition, Operation, etc. of the Special Prosecutor Candidate 
Recommendation Committee,” which was under review in a competence dispute case 
pending before the Court, there are circumstances suggesting that Respondent needed time to 
examine the appropriateness and implications of requesting such recommendations from the 
committee. Further, there is no evidence or objective material supporting Petitioner’s 
allegation that Respondent’s failure to request the recommendation of special prosecutor 
candidates delayed the related investigation or enabled accomplices to escape or evidence to 
be destroyed. Considering also that Article 3, Section (1) of the Act on the Appointment of 
Special Prosecutors, etc. (hereinafter referred to as the “Special Prosecutor Act”) requires 
action “without delay,” but does not provide any further definition or standard for that phrase, 
and that there was at the time no consensus in precedent, academic opinion, or judicial cases 
regarding the matter, we cannot find that Respondent violated, inter alia, Articles 7, 66, and 
71 of the Constitution, Article 3, Section (1) of the Special Prosecutor Act, or Article 56 of 
the State Public Officials Act.
(e) Regarding the failure to appoint Constitutional Court Justices 
The President has a constitutional duty under Article 111, Section (2) of the Constitution to 
appoint as Justices the persons elected by the National Assembly under Article 111, Section 
(3), unless such persons fail to meet the qualifications prescribed by the Constitution and the 
Constitutional Court Act, or there is a defect in the election process in violation of the 
Constitution —which embodies the principle of parliamentary democracy —or of statutes such 
as the National Assembly Act. Respondent, who came to act as President, is likewise bound 
by such a constitutional duty to act. The three Constitutional Court Justices elected by the 
5National Assembly satisfied the qualifications prescribed by the Constitution and the 
Constitutional Court Act, and there is no ground to regard their election process as defective 
on account of any violation of the Constitution or statutes, such as the National Assembly Act. 
Therefore, Respondent bore a specific constitutional duty to appoint the three persons elected 
by the National Assembly as Justices. However, even before receiving the official notification 
from the National Assembly of the election of the Justices, and amid intense political conflict 
between the ruling and opposition parties over that election, Respondent expressed, inter alia, 
at a State Council meeting and in a public statement, that he would condition the 
appointments upon bipartisan agreement, thereby definitively declaring in advance his refusal 
to appoint the three persons elected by the National Assembly as Justices. By doing so, 
Respondent violated his specific constitutional duty to act. Therefore, Respondent violated, 
inter alia, Articles 66 and 111 of the Constitution and Article 56 of the State Public Officials 
Act. This constitutes a ground for impeachment under the Constitution as an instance of “a 
violation of the Constitution or statutes in the performance of official duties.”
(2) Whether Respondent should be removed from office 
Even if Respondent, as Acting President, violated Articles 66 and 111 of the Constitution 
and Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act by refusing to appoint the Constitutional 
Court Justices, there is no evidence or objective material sufficient to establish that such 
refusal was motivated by an intent or purpose to incapacitate the Court in its impeachment 
trial of the sitting President. Moreover, given, inter alia, that political conflict over the 
appointment of Constitutional Court Justices was ongoing at the time and that controversies 
persisted concerning the role and scope, inter alia, of Respondent’s authority as Acting 
President following the National Assembly’s adoption of the impeachment resolution against 
the sitting President, Respondent’s violations of the Constitution and statutes cannot be 
regarded as having betrayed the public trust indirectly vested in him through the President as 
the appointing authority. Therefore, there are no grounds to justify the decision to remove 
Respondent from office. 
B. Opinion of rejection by Justice Kim Bok-hyeong
I also do not believe that Respondent violated the Constitution or statutes in relation to the 
alleged failure to appoint the Justices, for the following reasons.
6Although the President has a duty to act in exercising the authority to appoint 
Constitutional Court Justices, it is difficult to interpret this as an obligation to appoint them 
“immediately” after their election by the National Assembly. The time frame for exercising 
the President’s appointment authority should reasonably be understood as being “within a 
reasonable period,” taking into account, inter alia, the time necessary to carefully verify 
whether the Justices elected by the National Assembly meet the qualifications prescribed by 
the Constitution and the Constitutional Court Act, or whether there are defects in the election 
process that violate the Constitution, the National Assembly Act, or other statutes. 
Respondent’s concrete duty to act arises only after receiving notification from the National 
Assembly of the election of the Justices. However, Respondent never expressed an intention 
to refuse to appoint the three persons elected by the National Assembly as Justices during the 
period between the notification of their election and the adoption of the impeachment 
resolution against Respondent. Therefore, Respondent cannot be regarded as having 
conclusively expressed in advance an intention not to appoint the three elected persons as 
Justices. Further, given, inter alia, the circumstances in which, on December 26, 2024, at 
approximately 14:56, the election proposal for the three Constitutional Court Justices was 
passed by the National Assembly and notification of the election was sent to the President, 
and then, on the following day, December 27, 2024, at approximately 16:37, the 
impeachment resolution against Respondent was immediately adopted, it is difficult to 
conclude that a reasonable period had elapsed to examine, inter alia, whether the elected 
Justices met the qualifications or whether there had been any violations of the Constitution or 
the National Assembly Act in the election process. Accordingly, Respondent’s failure to 
appoint the Justices cannot be regarded as a violation of Articles 66 and 111 of the 
Constitution or Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act.
C. Opinion of upholding by Justice Chung Kyesun
I believe that not only Respondent’s failure to appoint a Constitutional Court Justice but 
also the delay in requesting recommendations for candidates for special prosecutor constitutes 
a violation of the Constitution or statutes, and that such violation is sufficiently grave to 
justify Respondent’s removal from office. 
If the request for recommending candidates for special prosecutor is delayed, the 
7legislative purpose of the Special Prosecutor Act—to ensure that investigations are conducted 
as fairly and efficiently as possible through the immediate appointment of a special 
prosecutor when an incident subject to investigation arises —would be undermined. Further, 
before the Court rendered its decision on the constitutionality of the provisions of the “Rule 
on the Composition, Operation, etc. of the Special Prosecutor Candidate Recommendation 
Committee,” Respondent prematurely presumed their unconstitutionality and failed to 
perform the legal duty explicitly prescribed in the Special Prosecutor Act. Such failure cannot 
be regarded as justified. Therefore, while serving as Acting President, Respondent’s failure to 
request the Recommendation Committee to recommend candidates for special prosecutor 
violated not only Article 3, Section (1) of the Special Prosecutor Act, but also Article 7, 
Section (1) and Article 66 of the Constitution and Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act. 
Next, I turn to the question of whether Respondent should be removed from office. As 
Prime Minister serving as Acting President, Respondent had a duty to minimize unnecessary 
controversy and promptly restore order amid national turmoil caused by the President’s 
suspension from duties. Nevertheless, through the aforementioned violations of the 
Constitution and statutes, Respondent instead exacerbated controversy and disorder, and 
further caused a constitutional crisis by disabling the Constitutional Court from performing 
its normal role and functions. Therefore, Respondent’s violations of the Constitution and 
statutes are sufficiently grave to justify removal from office. 
On balance, the benefits of protecting the Constitution by removing Respondent from 
office —namely, safeguarding the Constitution by withdrawing the people’s confidence 
indirectly conferred through the appointing authority, the President —overwhelmingly 
outweigh the national loss that would result from Respondent’s removal. Therefore, 
Respondent must be removed from office.
Summary of Dismissal Opinion of Justices Cheong Hyungsik and Cho Hanchang
The Acting President performs the “official duties of the President” on behalf of the 
President in an emergency situation arising from the President’s vacancy or incapacity in 
order to prevent a vacuum in presidential duties and a paralysis of national functions. Thus, 
the status of the Acting President should be regarded as “equivalent to that of the President.” 
Therefore, the requirements for the impeachment of an Acting President should be the same 
8as those applicable to the impeachment of the President.
Moreover, in a case such as the present one, where the grounds for impeachment of an 
Acting President exercising presidential duties also encompass grounds for impeachment 
related to the performance of official duties as Prime Minister, it is reasonable to apply the 
voting requirement for the impeachment of the President under the proviso of Article 65, 
Section (2) of the Constitution —namely, the approval of at least two-thirds of the total 
members of the National Assembly —to determine whether the impeachment resolution is 
adopted. This interpretation aligns with the purpose of the above constitutional provision, 
which requires a higher voting threshold for the President to ensure prudent exercise of the 
impeachment power. Furthermore, under Article 86, Section (1) of the Constitution, the 
Prime Minister is appointed by the President, who is directly elected by the people, with the 
consent of the National Assembly, which may likewise be considered a representative body 
of the people. Therefore, the democratic legitimacy or constitutional significance of the office 
of Prime Minister cannot be regarded as negligible. Consequently, the impeachment of a 
Prime Minister serving as Acting President should be interpreted to require the same degree 
of prudence as the impeachment of the President.
Because Petitioner adopted the impeachment resolution against Respondent with the 
approval of only a majority of all its members, the instant petition for an impeachment trial, 
based on such resolution, fails to satisfy the voting requirement prescribed in the proviso of 
Article 65, Section (2) of the Constitution and is therefore nonjusticiable.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
